revision.text:
[[File:Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG|thumb|right|Torii at Watatsu-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Watatsu-jinja'''|度津神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Sado, Niigata]], which is an island in the [[Sea of Japan]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
==History== The shrine was established before the 10th century.<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi Hamochi]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named {{nihongo|Isonotakeru no mikoto|五十猛命||}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> Isonotakeru is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''yabusame'') takes place at the branch Hachimangū of Watatsu Shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Watatsu was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Sado Province]] on [[Sado Island]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.
==References== {{reflist}}
==Other websites == {{Commons category-inline|Watatsu-jinja}}
<!-- {{coord|_|_|_|N|136|_|_|E|source:___|display=title}} -->
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan]]
[[en:Watatsu-jinja]] [[ja:度津神社]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = {{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine | type = | dedication = | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | location_map = Japan | coordinates ={{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | address = | phone = | website = }} {{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Sado, Niigata]], an island in the [[Sea of Japan]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>

B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Watatsu was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Sado Province]] on [[Sado Island]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
In the system of ranked Shinto shrines, Watatsu was listed among the third class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.{{fact|date=January 2013}}

In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.

[[File:Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG|thumb|right|Torii at Watatsu-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Watatsu-jinja'''|度津神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Sado, Niigata]], which is an island in the [[Sea of Japan]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
==History== The shrine was established before the 10th century.<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi Hamochi] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070830070528/http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi |date=2007-08-30 }}; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named {{nihongo|Isonotakeru no mikoto|五十猛命||}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> Isonotakeru is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''yabusame'') takes place at the branch Hachimangū of Watatsu Shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Watatsu was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Sado Province]] on [[Sado Island]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.

revision.text:
[[File:Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG|thumb|right|Torii at Watatsu-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Watatsu-jinja'''|度津神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Sado, Niigata]], which is an island in the [[Sea of Japan]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
==History== The shrine was established before the 10th century.<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi Hamochi] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070830070528/http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi |date=2007-08-30 }}; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named {{nihongo|Isonotakeru no mikoto|五十猛命||}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> Isonotakeru is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''yabusame'') takes place at the branch Hachimangū of Watatsu Shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"]{{Dead link|date=October 2021 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Watatsu was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Sado Province]] on [[Sado Island]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.

[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命 |}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar Tachibana Mitsuyoshi. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}}. <ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=4901398067}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watatsu jinja 1st Gate.JPG|Torii File:Watatsu jinja Honden.JPG|''Honden'' File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG|''Heiden'' File:Watatsu jinja Sanmon.JPG|Gate </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category|Watatsu-jinja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/8800 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.visitsado.com/spot/detail0126/ Sado tourist information] {{in lang|ja}}

Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命 |}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru [[Category:Beppo Shrines]]
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar Tachibana Mitsuyoshi. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=4901398067}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar Tachibana Mitsuyoshi. revision.comment: MathXplore moved page [[Watatsu-jinja]] to [[Watatsu Shrine]]: per enwiki
revision.text:
[[File:Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG|thumb|right|Torii at Watatsu-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Watatsu-jinja'''|度津神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Sado, Niigata]], which is an island in the [[Sea of Japan]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
==History== The shrine was established before the 10th century.<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi Hamochi] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070830070528/http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi |date=2007-08-30 }}; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named {{nihongo|Isonotakeru no mikoto|五十猛命||}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> Isonotakeru is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''yabusame'') takes place at the branch Hachimangū of Watatsu Shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"]{{Dead link|date=October 2021 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Watatsu was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Sado Province]] on [[Sado Island]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.
==References== {{reflist}}
==Other websites == {{Commons category-inline|Watatsu-jinja}}
<!-- {{coord|_|_|_|N|136|_|_|E|source:___|display=title}} -->
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命 |}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar Tachibana Mitsuyoshi. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

[[File:Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG|thumb|right|Torii at Watatsu-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Watatsu-jinja'''|度津神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Sado, Niigata]], which is an island in the [[Sea of Japan]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
==History== The shrine was established before the 10th century.<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi Hamochi] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070830070528/http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi |date=2007-08-30 }}; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named {{nihongo|Isonotakeru no mikoto|五十猛命||}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> Isonotakeru is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''yabusame'') takes place at the branch Hachimangū of Watatsu Shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"]{{Dead link|date=October 2021 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Watatsu was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Sado Province]] on [[Sado Island]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.

B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> Isonotakeru is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>

It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>

In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命 |}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar Tachibana Mitsuyoshi. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

[[File:Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG|thumb|right|Torii at Watatsu-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Watatsu-jinja'''|度津神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Sado, Niigata]], which is an island in the [[Sea of Japan]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
==History== The shrine was established before the 10th century.<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi Hamochi] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070830070528/http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi |date=2007-08-30 }}; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named {{nihongo|Isonotakeru no mikoto|五十猛命||}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> Isonotakeru is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''yabusame'') takes place at the branch Hachimangū of Watatsu Shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"]{{Dead link|date=October 2021 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Watatsu was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Sado Province]] on [[Sado Island]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.

The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命 |}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}, sister of Isonotakeru [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar Tachibana Mitsuyoshi. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''[[Oyahitsuhime no mikoto]]'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''[[Tsumatsuhime no mikoto]]'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar Tachibana Mitsuyoshi. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

[[File:Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG|thumb|right|Torii at Watatsu-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Watatsu-jinja'''|度津神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Sado, Niigata]], which is an island in the [[Sea of Japan]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
==History== The shrine was established before the 10th century.<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi Hamochi] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070830070528/http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi |date=2007-08-30 }}; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named {{nihongo|Isonotakeru no mikoto|五十猛命||}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> Isonotakeru is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships.<ref>Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''yabusame'') takes place at the branch Hachimangū of Watatsu Shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"]{{Dead link|date=October 2021 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
Watatsu was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Sado Province]] on [[Sado Island]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|大屋津姫命}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|抓津姫命}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar Tachibana Mitsuyoshi. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>

The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ooyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja| オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ooyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja| オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar [[Tachibana]] Mitsuyoshi. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ooyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja| オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ooyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja| オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜}}. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watatsu jinja 1st Gate.JPG|Torii File:Watatsu jinja Honden.JPG|''Honden'' File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG|''Heiden'' File:Watatsu jinja Sanmon.JPG|Gate </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category|Watatsu-jinja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/8800 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.visitsado.com/spot/detail0126/ Sado tourist information] {{in lang|ja}}

Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ooyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja| オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ooyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja| オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜}}. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = Watatsu jinja 2nd Gate.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|51|36.9|N|138|19|50.3|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Isonotakeru no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of [[Sado, Niigata]].<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sado Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ooyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja| オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ooyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja| オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[Sado island|Sado]] in the [[Sea of Japan]], the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the [[Kan'ei]] era (1624-1643) of the [[Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927年）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Chūai]] (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the [[Honma clan]], who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜}}. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from [[Ogi, Niigata|Ogi Port]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watatsu jinja 1st Gate.JPG|Torii File:Watatsu jinja Honden.JPG|''Honden'' File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG|''Heiden'' File:Watatsu jinja Sanmon.JPG|Gate </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category|Watatsu-jinja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/8800 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.visitsado.com/spot/detail0126/ Sado tourist information] {{in lang|ja}}
