{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado, Niigata}}.<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watatsu jinja 1st Gate.JPG|Torii File:Watatsu jinja Honden.JPG|''Honden'' File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG|''Heiden'' File:Watatsu jinja Sanmon.JPG|Gate </gallery>

It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado, Niigata}}.<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado, Niigata}}.<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watatsu jinja 1st Gate.JPG|Torii File:Watatsu jinja Honden.JPG|''Honden'' File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG|''Heiden'' File:Watatsu jinja Sanmon.JPG|Gate </gallery>

==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.8602|N|138.331|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Isotakeru|WD=Q10881556}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Sado|WD=Q499047}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado, Niigata}}.<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.8602|N|138.331|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Isotakeru|WD=Q10881556}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Sado|WD=Q499047}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado, Niigata}}.<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watatsu jinja 1st Gate.JPG|Torii File:Watatsu jinja Honden.JPG|''Honden'' File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG|''Heiden'' File:Watatsu jinja Sanmon.JPG|Gate </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category|Watatsu-jinja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/8800 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.visitsado.com/spot/detail0126/ Sado tourist information] {{in lang|ja}} ]]]]{{Isotakeru Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}

The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port|1=Ogi, Niigata}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.8602|N|138.331|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Isotakeru|WD=Q10881556|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Sado|WD=Q499047|1=Sado, Niigata}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado, Niigata|1=Sado, Niigata}}.<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province|1=Sado Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan|1=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port|1=Ogi, Niigata}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.8602|N|138.331|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Isotakeru|WD=Q10881556|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Sado|WD=Q499047|1=Sado, Niigata}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado, Niigata|1=Sado, Niigata}}.<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province|1=Sado Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan|1=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port|1=Ogi, Niigata}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watatsu jinja 1st Gate.JPG|Torii File:Watatsu jinja Honden.JPG|''Honden'' File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG|''Heiden'' File:Watatsu jinja Sanmon.JPG|Gate </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category|Watatsu-jinja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/8800 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.visitsado.com/spot/detail0126/ Sado tourist information] {{in lang|ja}} ]]]]{{Isotakeru Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan|1=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port|1=Ogi, Niigata}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watatsu jinja 1st Gate.JPG|Torii File:Watatsu jinja Honden.JPG|''Honden'' File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG|''Heiden'' File:Watatsu jinja Sanmon.JPG|Gate </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Watatsu-jinja]][[en:Watatsu Shrine]][[fr:Watatsu-jinja]][[ja:度津神社]][[simple:Watatsu Shrine]][[zh:度津神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Watatsu-jinja]][[en:Watatsu Shrine]][[fr:Watatsu-jinja]][[ja:度津神社]][[simple:Watatsu Shrine]][[zh:度津神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Watatsu Shrine]][[fr:Watatsu-jinja]][[ja:度津神社]][[simple:Watatsu Shrine]][[zh:度津神社]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Watatsu Shrine |native_name = 度津神社 | image = File:Watatsu-jinja.JPG | caption = Torii at Watatsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.8602|N|138.331|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Watasu Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Isotakeru|WD=Q10881556|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 550-4 Hamochiiioka, Sado-shi, Niigata-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 23 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Sado|WD=Q499047|1=Sado, Niigata}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Watatsu Shrine'''|度津神社|Watatsu-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Hamochiiioka neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado, Niigata|1=Sado, Niigata}}.<ref>VisitSado.com, [http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml Watatsu Shrine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304121858/http://www.visitsado.com/en/01outline/08pt_09/06.shtml |date=2016-03-04 }}; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province|1=Sado Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Watatsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}'''|五十猛命|}}, god shipbuilding and sailing.<ref name="Watatsu Jinja">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/watatsu-jinja/ "Watatsu Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref><ref name="books.google.com">Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA372&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 372].</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Oyahitsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|大屋津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|lt=Tsumatsuhime no mikoto|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime|qq=}}'''|抓津姫命||}}, sister of Isonotakeru
==History== Located on the island of [[:en:Sado island|Sado]] in the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}}, the history of Watatsu Shrine is uncertain, as the shrine was completely destroyed by a flood during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624-1643) of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>Sado Travel Guide, [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~kagetsu/englishyoyaku/travelguideplace.html#hamochi "Hamochi"]; 佐渡市 観光課 観光振興係 (Sado Tourism Promotion), 佐渡一ノ宮度津神社 (Sado Ichinomiya Shrine); excerpt, 度津神社の由緒は「延喜式」神名帳（927th year ）に記載されている神社を式内社と言い、当時佐渡の国には九社あり; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref> According to shrine tradition, it was founded by the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}} (reigned 192–200 AD). Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD, but only as a minor shrine. It was destroyed by a flood in 1470, and rebuilt by the {{ill|Honma clan|en|Honma clan|WD=Q2724497|lt=Honma clan|1=Honma clan}}, who were rulers of Sado island, in 1472. The shrine appears to have been destroyed repeatedly by floods in 1493, 1533 and 1593, and was merged in 1493 with a nearby [[Hachiman shrine]]. It is only mentioned as an ''ichinomiya'' in 1678 with the publication the {{nihongo|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record|諸国一宮巡詣記|Shokoku Ichinomiya Pilgrimage Record}} by Shinto scholar {{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544184}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} in 1872, the shrine was classified as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}.<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|'''Isonotakeru no mikoto'''|五十猛命||}},<ref name="books.google.com"/> the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], who is said to have taught people shipbuilding and how to use ships,<ref name="Watatsu Jinja"/> and his two sisters {{nihongo|'''Oyahitsuhime no mikoto'''|大屋津姫命 ||}} and {{nihongo|'''Tsumatsuhime no mikoto'''|抓津姫命 ||}}<ref name="Shibuya"/>
Each year in late-April, horseback archery (''[[yabusame]]'') takes place at the branch shrine in the town of Hamochi.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=996 "''Yabusame shinji'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-11-6.</ref>
The shrine is located about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Ogi, Niigata|en|Ogi, Niigata|WD=Q11460942|lt=Ogi Port|1=Ogi, Niigata}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
